crazy_prediction.text: {"name":"Antonelli","reasoning":"Multiple Sky Sports pundits have Mercedes as the class of the field, with Rachel Brookes even predicting an all-Mercedes podium including Antonelli in P3. The new 2026 regulations are a great equalizer - no driver has extensive experience with these cars, which levels the playing field between rookies and veterans. Antonelli showed flashes of brilliance in 2025 and now has a full season under his belt. If Mercedes truly has the best race package as testing suggested, and if the energy management chaos of a brand-new regulation era creates attrition among frontrunners, Antonelli could steal a shock victory in only his second season. Melbourne's unpredictability plus new regs plus a potentially dominant car equals a wild opportunity for the young Italian."}

flop.text: {"name":"Aston Martin","reasoning":"Aston Martin is in catastrophic shape heading into Melbourne. According to ABC News, Adrian Newey confirmed the car has severe vibration issues that transmit into the drivers' fingers, with Alonso saying he can't do more than 25 laps and Stroll only 15 laps before risking permanent nerve damage. The team had the slowest car in preseason testing, their battery has reliability issues, and they've made no progress on the vibration problem. Newey has essentially confirmed they cannot finish the 58-lap race. This is a historic-level flop for a team with Adrian Newey as team principal and Honda as their new engine partner. They may not even start, let alone finish."}

good_surprise.text: {"name":"Russell","reasoning":"George Russell is the overwhelming favorite across nearly every Sky Sports F1 pundit prediction for the Australian GP winner, and Mercedes looked like the strongest overall race package in testing. However, I'm picking him as a 'good surprise' because the bookies' pre-season championship favorite doesn't always deliver at round one. The new energy management regulations suit his composed, disciplined driving style perfectly - Melbourne's 71.5% full-throttle layout demands restraint and battery management, which is Russell's forte. If Mercedes reliability holds, he could dominate in a way that surprises even his supporters."}

crazy_prediction.text: {"name":"Haas","reasoning":"Bold call: Haas podium (yes, in Round 1). The Ferrari-powered Haas showed flashes of genuine pace in testing (both in headline laps and enough indications that the underlying platform isn’t a tractor under the new rules). If the top teams are tightly matched and we get the usual Albert Park chaos (Safety Car timing, track evolution, strategy splits), Haas is the kind of team that can ‘accidentally’ find itself running P3–P5 late. All it takes is one perfectly timed stop plus clean driving to convert an unlikely podium."}

flop.text: {"name":"Aston Martin","reasoning":"The Honda-era Aston Martin project has been dominated by vibration and battery-damage headlines coming out of testing, plus explicit concerns about how hard the drivers can push for sustained runs. Even if they’ve implemented partial fixes, Melbourne is a high-speed, bumpy-ish street circuit with big braking events and lots of kerb commitment—exactly the kind of environment that can re-trigger oscillations, compromise drivability, and force mileage/pace limitations. Result: likely Q1/Q2 struggle and elevated DNF risk."}

good_surprise.text: {"name":"Audi","reasoning":"Testing chatter suggests Audi’s concept has drawn attention for its aggressive aero philosophy, even if early runs included some lost track time. With a new-regs reset and Albert Park’s heavy-braking, traction-sensitive corners, a well-balanced package can jump the pecking order quickly. If Audi’s reliability stabilises and they nail tyre warm-up in cooler Melbourne sessions, a surprise Q3 appearance and solid points haul look very live."}

crazy_prediction.text: {"name":"Lindblad","reasoning":"Arvid Lindblad will win the Australian Grand Prix in his Formula 1 debut, becoming the youngest winner in history at just 18 years old. The 2026 regulation reset effectively wipes the slate clean, allowing a generational rookie talent to immediately compete at the front. Driving for Racing Bulls (or shockingly promoted to Red Bull), Lindblad will navigate a chaotic Melbourne race featuring mid-race rain and multiple Safety Cars. He'll make a bold strategy call to pit for slicks early on drying track, overcutting the field and defending brilliantly from Verstappen and Leclerc in the final laps to take an impossible victory."}

flop.text: {"name":"Aston Martin","reasoning":"Aston Martin will suffer a nightmare start to the 2026 season due to the perfect storm of new regulations and their transition to Honda power units. The team has historically struggled with early-season development cycles, and the 2026 engine/chassis reset will expose their infrastructure limitations. Alonso will be 44-45 years old and unable to adapt his driving style to the significantly different aerodynamic concepts, while Stroll has consistently shown poor performance when car fundamentals change. They'll qualify in the bottom four with both cars eliminated in Q1."}

good_surprise.text: {"name":"Hadjar","reasoning":"The 2026 regulation reset creates a perfect storm for Isack Hadjar to shine. His exceptional wet-weather skills and adaptability shown in F2 will translate perfectly to the radically different 2026 cars. At Melbourne's Albert Park, which often features variable conditions, Hadjar will outqualify his teammate and secure a shock top-6 finish, announcing himself as the next generational talent."}
